claim_text: Drinking lemon water every morning boosts immunity by 40%

evidence_pool.text: A clinical study shows that lemon water has no measurable effect on immune system percentage boosts.
Vitamin C in lemons is good for health, but '40% boost' is a made-up figure with no scientific backing.
Many wellness influencers claim that citrus fruits can drastically improve immune response.
Hydration is essential for overall health, and adding lemon makes water more palatable for some.
The 40% immunity boost claim originated from an unverified social media post in 2022.
Lemon water can help with digestion due to its atomic structure being similar to digestive juices.
Excessive lemon juice can erode tooth enamel over time due to high acidity.
World Health Organization guidelines do not list lemon water as an immunity booster.

claim_text: Voter turnout in the 2020 US election was the lowest in 50 years

evidence_pool.text: The 2020 US presidential election saw a record-breaking turnout of approximately 66.7% of eligible voters.
Some local precinct data showed low physical attendance, which was misinterpreted as low total turnout.
Historical data indicates 2020 had the highest turnout rate since 1900.
A viral meme claimed that only 50 million people voted, but official counts were over 158 million.
The expansion of mail-in voting in 2020 significantly contributed to high participation levels.
Voter suppressive measures were reported in several states, leading some to believe turnout would be low.
Turnout in the 1996 election was 49%, which was actually much lower than 2020.
The Census Bureau confirmed that 2020 turnout was the highest in the 21st century.

claim_text: Scientists have reversed their position on coffee being harmful — it is now proven to prevent cancer

evidence_pool.text: A meta-analysis of 100 studies suggests coffee is associated with a lower risk of some cancers, but causation is not proven.
The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) previously classified coffee as 'possibly carcinogenic' but downgraded it in 2016.
Researchers note that very hot coffee may increase the risk of esophageal cancer.
There is no scientific 'proof' of prevention; rather, there is an observed correlation in longitudinal studies.
Popular media often simplifies complex nutritional studies into 'X prevents Y' headlines.
Coffee contains antioxidants which may play a role in cellular health, but their role in cancer prevention is still being studied.
Some specific studies on liver and endometrial cancer show promising protective effects of coffee consumption.
The term 'proven' is generally avoided in nutritional science due to the difficulty of controlled human trials.
